project_identification.lead_agency.answer: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.cross_streets.answer: East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road

project_location.within_2_miles_waterway.answer: American River; Willow Creek drainage channel

project_location.within_2_miles_airports.answer: Mather Airport (former Mather Air Force Base)

project_location.within_2_miles_schools.answer: Riverbend Elementary School; American River Charter High School; Willow Creek Middle School

local_action_type.other_local_action_typ.answer: Development Agreement approval; Tree Removal Permit; Grading Permit

development_type.educational.answer: No new standalone schools; on-site early childhood learning center (approximately 6,000 sq ft) integrated into the mixed-use village to support families residing in the project and surrounding neighborhoods.

development_type.recreational.answer: Approximately 7.2 acres of public and private recreational amenities including a central 3.5-acre neighborhood park with playgrounds, multi-use lawn, picnic areas, and shade structures; a 1.2-mile multi-use trail along the Willow Creek drainage with fitness stations; and smaller pocket plazas with seating, public art, and outdoor dining terraces.

development_type.transportation_type.answer: On-site local street network, including new signalized intersection at East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road; Class II and Class IV bicycle facilities; enhanced pedestrian crossings; and a new bus stop with shelter and pull-out for Sacramento Regional Transit bus service.

development_type.other_development_type.answer: Public infrastructure improvements (stormwater detention basin, upgraded water and sewer lines, and undergrounding of existing overhead utilities) and installation of rooftop solar panels on commercial and office buildings (approximately 750 kW total capacity).

project_issues_discussed_in_document.oth.answer: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and equitable access to new amenities; construction-related vibration impacts to nearby residences; and climate change resilience, including urban heat island reduction and flood risk adaptation.

land_use_and_project_description.present.answer:
Existing Land Use: Predominantly fallow irrigated pasture with scattered remnant orchard trees and a disused metal equipment shed; portions of the site are currently used informally for dog walking and unauthorized dirt-bike activity.

Zoning: Primarily AR-10 (Agricultural-Residential, 10-acre minimum) with a small portion designated RD-5 (Residential, 5 units/acre) along East Riverbend Boulevard.

General Plan Designation: LDR (Low Density Residential) with a corridor overlay along East Riverbend Boulevard identified for future mixed-use and neighborhood-serving commercial uses in the City of Riverbend 2035 General Plan.

land_use_and_project_description.project.answer:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a comprehensive, master-planned infill development on approximately 48.6 acres in the unincorporated community of Riverbend, east of the City of Sacramento. The project is proposed by Riverbend Village Partners, LLC, in coordination with the City of Riverbend Community Development Department, to transform largely underutilized agricultural land into a walkable, transit-supportive, mixed-use neighborhood that provides housing, jobs, and neighborhood-serving retail.

Key components of the project include:

1. Residential Development (Approximately 312 Units)
- 132 single-family detached homes on individual lots, generally ranging from 3,000 to 4,500 square feet, with a mix of one- and two-story floor plans targeting young families and move-down empty nesters.
- 96 townhome units in three-story buildings fronting internal streets and the central park, designed with stoops and porches to activate the public realm.
- 84 multifamily apartment units in two mixed-use buildings above ground-floor retail and office space along East Riverbend Boulevard, intended to provide smaller, more affordable rental options for singles, seniors, and service workers.
- A minimum of 15 percent of the total units (approximately 47 units) will be deed-restricted as affordable to low- and moderate-income households, distributed across the townhome and multifamily components to avoid concentrating affordable units in a single area.

2. Commercial and Office Development
- Approximately 82,000 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial uses, including a small-format grocery store (approximately 25,000 square feet), cafes and restaurants with outdoor seating, a pharmacy, personal services (e.g., barber, nail salon, dry cleaner), and small local retailers.
- Approximately 65,000 square feet of flexible office space in two- to three-story buildings, anticipated to accommodate medical offices, professional services (e.g., accountants, insurance brokers, small tech firms), and co-working space. The project team has had preliminary conversations with a local pediatric clinic and a small engineering firm interested in locating in the project.
- Ground-floor commercial spaces will be oriented to the internal main street and East Riverbend Boulevard, with transparent storefronts, wide sidewalks, street trees, and pedestrian amenities.

3. Parks, Open Space, and Recreational Amenities
- A centrally located 3.5-acre neighborhood park featuring a large multi-purpose turf field, age-separated playgrounds, a shaded picnic grove with BBQs, a small splash pad, a community garden with raised planters and tool storage, and a restroom/storage building.
- A linear greenway and multi-use trail along the Willow Creek drainage channel on the eastern edge of the site, providing a 1.2-mile loop connection with fitness stations, interpretive signage about the creek’s ecology, and native riparian plantings.
- Several smaller pocket parks and plazas distributed throughout the residential neighborhoods and the mixed-use main street, including seating, public art installations by local artists, and flexible hardscape areas that can accommodate food trucks, farmers’ markets, and neighborhood events.

4. Transportation and Circulation Improvements
- A new signalized intersection at East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road, with dedicated turn lanes and enhanced crosswalks, to provide the primary vehicular access to the project.
- A fine-grained internal street network designed on a modified grid, with traffic-calming features such as bulb-outs, raised crosswalks, and narrow travel lanes to encourage low vehicle speeds and prioritize pedestrian and bicycle safety.
- Class II bike lanes on East Riverbend Boulevard and Willow Creek Road frontages, and a combination of Class II and Class IV (protected) bike facilities on key internal streets, providing connections to nearby schools, parks, and existing regional bikeways along the American River.
- A new Sacramento Regional Transit bus stop with a shelter, seating, lighting, and a bus pull-out along East Riverbend Boulevard, coordinated with RT to serve existing Route 74 and potential future service enhancements.

5. Utilities, Infrastructure, and Sustainability Features
- Extension and upsizing of existing water, sewer, and storm drain lines in East Riverbend Boulevard to serve the project, consistent with the City’s adopted master plans.
- On-site stormwater treatment and detention facilities, including bioretention planters integrated into streetscapes and parking lot landscapes, and a central detention basin designed with gently sloped sides and native plantings to provide both flood control and habitat value.
- Undergrounding of existing overhead utility lines along the project’s East Riverbend Boulevard frontage to improve visual quality and reliability.
- Installation of rooftop solar photovoltaic systems on all commercial and office buildings (approximately 750 kW total capacity) and pre-wiring of all residential units for future solar installation and electric vehicle charging.
- Implementation of a transportation demand management (TDM) program that may include subsidized transit passes for residents and employees during initial occupancy, secure bicycle parking, car-share spaces, and an online ride-matching portal for office tenants.

6. Land Use Entitlements and Regulatory Actions
- General Plan Amendment to change the existing Low Density Residential (LDR) designation to a Mixed Use Neighborhood (MU-N) designation, consistent with the City’s long-term vision for a more compact, walkable development pattern along the East Riverbend Boulevard corridor.
- Rezone from AR-10 and RD-5 to a new Planned Development (PD) zoning district tailored to the mixed-use program, incorporating form-based standards for building placement, height, and frontage types.
- Adoption of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan, which will establish detailed land use, circulation, infrastructure, and design guidelines for the project area.
- Approval of a Tentative Subdivision Map to create residential, commercial, office, park, open space, and right-of-way parcels, as well as individual single-family lots.
- Approval of a Planned Unit Development (PUD) permit, site plan and design review, and a conditional use permit for certain commercial uses (e.g., drive-through pharmacy, outdoor dining with amplified music) subject to performance standards.
- Negotiation and adoption of a Development Agreement between the City and the project applicant addressing phasing, financing of backbone infrastructure, affordable housing commitments, and community benefits such as park improvements and public art funding.

7. Phasing
The project is anticipated to be constructed in three primary phases over approximately 8–10 years, depending on market conditions:
- Phase 1: Backbone infrastructure, central neighborhood park, initial single-family lots, and first mixed-use building with ground-floor retail and upper-story apartments.
- Phase 2: Remaining single-family and townhome neighborhoods, additional commercial pads, and completion of the Willow Creek greenway and multi-use trail.
- Phase 3: Office buildings, second mixed-use building, final neighborhood plazas, and full build-out of the TDM program and solar installations.

Overall Objectives
The overarching objectives of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project are to:
- Provide a diverse mix of housing types and price points, including deed-restricted affordable units, to help address local and regional housing needs.
- Create a vibrant, walkable neighborhood where residents can meet daily needs (grocery, services, recreation) within a short walk or bike ride, reducing vehicle miles traveled and greenhouse gas emissions.
- Support local economic development by providing new space for small businesses, professional offices, and neighborhood-serving services, creating approximately 400–500 permanent jobs at full build-out.
- Enhance connectivity to nearby schools, parks, and the American River through improved pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure.
- Incorporate sustainability and resiliency measures such as energy-efficient building design, urban tree canopy expansion, low-impact development stormwater features, and climate-adaptive landscaping.

The Draft EIR evaluates potential environmental impacts associated with the project, identifies feasible mitigation measures, and analyzes alternatives, including a No Project Alternative and a Reduced Intensity Alternative that would develop fewer housing units and a smaller commercial component while retaining the core mixed-use, walkable neighborhood concept.
